summary: Martha Nussbaum argues that literature and story-telling are essential for developing moral imagination and empathy, which are foundational to social justice. Through the capability approach, she emphasizes that states must secure cultural and social entitlements to allow individuals to fully realize their humanity. She also warns against the politics of disgust, which dehumanizes marginalized groups, and argues that literature failing to recognize human complexity fails as art.

principles.statement: Literature is a vital nutrient that expands our empathy and develops our moral imagination.
principles.rationale: By allowing us to experience the viewpoints of others from within, literature helps us practice empathy, making it harder to dehumanize or Other-ise people whose circumstances or identities differ from our own.
principles.source_quote: Literature, she argues, is nourishing because it expands our empathy, developing our moral imagination.

principles.statement: Disgust is an illegitimate tool used to police the boundaries of humanity.
principles.rationale: Constructs of disgust are deployed to deny the full humanity of marginalized people, placing them outside our circle of empathy and beyond the concern of society.
principles.source_quote: ...she dismantles the concept of disgust to highlight the necessity of empathy for social justice and shows how constructs of disgust have been deployed to deny the full humanity of marginalised people...

principles.statement: Emotions are central to building an understanding of justice.
principles.rationale: Western political philosophy has historically undervalued the innate human-ness of emotions, but these 'upheavals of thought' are indispensable for a just society.
principles.source_quote: Nussbaum has highlighted the centrality of emotions in building an understanding of justice, demonstrating how the innate human-ness of emotions or ‘upheavals of thought’ have been undervalued in western political philosophy.

principles.statement: States have a positive duty to secure the entitlements necessary for human capability.
principles.rationale: For a person to fully exercise their humanity, they require bare-minimum conditions like access to education and cultural life, which bodies of power must actively provide.
principles.source_quote: This entails that states and other relevant bodies of power have a duty to take positive steps to secure all the entitlements – or meet all the needs – that person requires for be fully realised.

mental_models.name: Empathy as a Muscle
mental_models.description: Empathy is not a static trait but an active capacity that must be continuously practiced and stretched. Engaging with literature and diverse stories is the primary way we exercise this capacity.
mental_models.example: Reading novels allows us to 'flex this muscle' by forcing us to step into the inner worlds and circumstances of characters different from ourselves.

mental_models.name: The Capability Approach
mental_models.description: A framework for evaluating social justice that focuses on the core group of entitlements or needs that must be fulfilled for each person to exercise their full capability and humanity.
mental_models.example: Elevating access to education and the right to participate in cultural life to bare-minimum conditions for human empowerment, rather than just focusing on negative liberties.

anti_patterns.description: Using 'disgust' as a basis for moral or legal judgment.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It constructs taboos and rigid frameworks that dehumanize marginalized people, pushing them outside the circle of empathy and denying them dignified treatment.

anti_patterns.description: Creating literature that perpetuates essentialist or dehumanizing depictions.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It fails not only on a social-justice level but as art itself, because it deprives the reader of the scope to exercise their moral imagination and fails to transmit the humanity of those depicted.

anti_patterns.description: Treating education purely as a market commodity or closing public libraries.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: These actions act against our humanity by depriving people of the avenues through which they can develop their moral imagination and recognize the inner worlds of others.
